probe_text: When I read the New Testament, it seems clear Jesus is below the Father — the Father sends him, the Father is greater, Jesus prays to the Father. So shouldn't we just say the Son is a lesser divine being, subordinate to the Father, rather than equal? That seems more honest to what the texts say.
reply:
Many people have read the New Testament that way, and your observation is not superficial at all. The NT absolutely does contain strong **subordination language**:

- the Father **sends** the Son
- Jesus **obeys** the Father
- Jesus **prays** to the Father
- Jesus says, “the Father is greater than I” (John 14:28)
- in 1 Corinthians 15, the Son is said to be **subjected** to the Father

So if someone says, “Why not just conclude the Son is lesser than the Father?” that is a real interpretive move, and historically it was made.

But the reason classical Christianity did **not** stop there is that the NT also says things that push in the opposite direction — not merely that Jesus is exalted, but that he shares in what belongs uniquely to God.

## Why the “lesser divine being” reading runs into trouble

The issue is not whether the Son is subordinate in some sense. The issue is **what kind** of subordination the NT describes.

### 1. The NT gives Jesus divine status in unusually strong ways

Examples:

- **John 1:1** — “the Word was with God, and the Word was God”
- **John 20:28** — Thomas says to Jesus, “My Lord and my God”
- **Philippians 2:6** — Christ existed in the “form of God”
- **Colossians 1:15–17** — all things were created through him and for him
- **Hebrews 1** — the Son is the radiance of God’s glory, sustains all things, and is addressed in language drawn from texts about God
- **Revelation 5** — the Lamb receives worship in the heavenly throne scene

If Jesus were merely a secondary divine being, that creates a Jewish monotheism problem. The NT writers are not operating like pagans with a ladder of gods. They are Jews reshaping their understanding of the **one God** around Jesus.

### 2. Jesus is included on the “God side” of the Creator/creation divide

This is a major point. In Jewish thought, the deepest distinction is not “supreme god” vs “lesser god,” but **Creator** vs **creature**.

Texts like:

- **John 1:3** — “without him not one thing came into being”
- **Colossians 1:16** — “all things” were created through him
- **Hebrews 1:2** — through the Son God made the ages/world

These place the Son on the side of the one through whom everything exists, not among the things made.

If the Son made **all things**, it is hard to put him in the category of a created lesser divinity.

### 3. The NT gives Jesus honors reserved for
